exercises.title: enumerate() and zip()
exercises.description: enumerate() adds an index to iteration; zip() pairs up two sequences. Practice both.
exercises.starterCode:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
prices = [0.99, 0.59, 2.49]

# TODO: Use enumerate() to print a numbered list (starting at 1)
print("Fruit list:")
for index, fruit in enumerate(fruits, start=1):
    print(f"  {index}. {fruit}")

print()

# TODO: Use zip() to pair fruits with prices
print("Price list:")
for fruit, price in zip(fruits, prices):
    print(f"  {fruit}: ${price:.2f}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Fruit list:
  1. apple
  2. banana
  3. cherry

Price list:
  apple: $0.99
  banana: $0.59
  cherry: $2.49

exercises.title: any(), all(), sum(), min(), max()
exercises.description: These built-ins work on iterables and return a single summary value.
exercises.starterCode:
scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 60, 88]
passing_threshold = 70

# TODO: Print whether all scores are above 50
print(all(s > 50 for s in scores))

# TODO: Print whether any score is above 90
print(any(s > 90 for s in scores))

# TODO: Print sum, min, and max of scores
print(sum(scores))
print(min(scores))
print(max(scores))

# TODO: Print the average
print(sum(scores) / len(scores))
hints: all() returns True only if ALL elements satisfy the condition.
any() returns True if AT LEAST ONE element satisfies the condition.
sum() adds all elements; min()/max() find extremes.

exercises.title: sorted() and reversed()
exercises.description: sorted() returns a new sorted list; reversed() returns an iterator in reverse order. Neither modifies the original.
exercises.starterCode:
words = ["banana", "apple", "cherry", "date", "elderberry"]

# TODO: Print words sorted alphabetically
print(sorted(words))

# TODO: Print words sorted by length (shortest first)
print(sorted(words, key=len))

# TODO: Print words sorted by length descending
print(sorted(words, key=len, reverse=True))

# TODO: Print words in reverse order (use reversed)
print(list(reversed(words)))

# TODO: Show original is unchanged
print(words)
hints: sorted() never changes the original list.
reversed() returns an iterator — wrap with list() to see it.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Built-in Functions
warmup.description: Python's built-ins include len, range, type, print, sum, min, max, sorted, enumerate, zip.
warmup.starterCode:
numbers = [4, 2, 7, 1, 9, 3]
print(len(numbers))
print(sum(numbers))
print(min(numbers))
print(max(numbers))
print(sorted(numbers))
